tweets.text:
I start with 100 rare oranges that never spoil. They are so rare these are the only 100 that will ever exist. 

I give 30 away and sell 20 to my friends. Of the remaining 50, I put half in my left pocket and half in my right.

Through business activity, I earn enough money to buy 5 oranges from the marketplace where the orange community has been trading them, among other rare fruits, and put them in my shirt pocket.

My goal is to increase the value of my fruit marketplace. What is my optimal choice?

1) launch 5 oranges into the sun from my left pocket
2) launch 5 oranges into the sun from my right pocket
3) launch 5 oranges into the sun from my shirt pocket

Discuss.

tweets.text:
Robinhood launched using Arbitrum tech, joining the Arbitrum stack (Orbit).

Yet $ARB has only barely bounced back from its ATL 7 days ago.

Massive win for Arbitrum from technical, reputational, and BD perspective but also from liquidity via Orbit but market doesn't care.

Years ago this would've pumped $ARB significantly.

But market now requires fruits from this partnership, ideally increased fees that accrue to $ARB.

At least Robinhood paid $1M to Arbitrum’s Open House 2026 program.

It's also a win for Ethereum:

Instead of Robinhood building their own corpo-slop L1, Robinhood stays within the Ethereum ecosystem with some fees being routed to the L1.

So Ethereum L2 roadmap didn't go to waste despite liquidity fragmentation and worse UX in the short run.

tweets.text:
J-Cal’s advice to founders: “If you partner with OpenAI or Anthropic, they will slit your throat and take your business wholesale. Don't trust them. Use your own models.”

“There is no free pizza. There's no free beer. When somebody like Sam Altman comes to you and says, ‘Here's some free tokens,’ your alarm should go off.

If you follow the Microsoft example, Lotus 1-2-3 and Word Perfect were their partners. They were replaced with Excel. They were replaced, obviously, with Microsoft Word. You don't even know those other two.

That's exactly what OpenAI has to do, and they have no choice but to do that now because they have a trillion-dollar market cap. They must win the application layer.

And Sam Altman went to Y Combinator and he said, ‘We'll give you $2 million worth of free tokens.’

And I came out and I said, ‘Listen, this is nothing personal against Sam. Sam's a very aggressive deal maker and he wants to get access to those startups because he knows, having run Y Combinator, that if he can get their innovations, those founders' latest thoughts about what's around the corner, he can incorporate them into the platform.’

Zuckerberg did the same thing. He said, ‘Hey, I'm going to give people a bunch of access, going to give them money. Come to the Facebook platform.’

Nobody who went to bed with Microsoft in the '80s, Facebook in the 2000s, or Sam Altman now in the 2020s did not wake up with their throat slit.

This is a message to founders. If you partner with any of these people, they will slit your throat and take your business wholesale.

There is nothing to discuss here. Don't trust them. Use your own models.”

Unfortunately, numerous tests by psychologists show that the majority of people follow the lower-level path most of the time, which leads to inferior decisions without their realizing it. As Carl Jung put it, "Until you make the unconscious conscious, it will direct your life and you will call it fate." It's even more important that decision making be evidence-based and logical when groups of people are working together. If it's not, the process will inevitably be dominated by the most powerful rather than the most insightful participants, which is not only unfair but suboptimal. Successful organizations have cultures in which evidence-based decision making is the norm rather than the exception.
#principleoftheday

tweets.text:
You obviously didn't say it like that, but it's what it comes down to for anyone who can talk freely about it without being afraid of upsetting anyone.

 What you said is:

- that the token exists to hedge out future compute prices => literally no one bought the token with the thesis that its only value was this, and *strictly* this

- that the token accrues value through buybacks with every net new subscription => we're talking about $3M worth of buybacks a year for a $1.5bn FDV coin, and the Venice team is completely free to stop these buybacks at any point in time (trust me bro)

- that they never said that the token was like equity and that anyone who believed that was stupid

But here's the thing, that's not how it works. When a protocol launches a token, the market will tend to automatically assume it's pseudo equity for the project, *especially* when they trust the founder and he's supposed to be super ethical/moral/fair etc. Btw we do now have a crystal clear example of such token with HYPE, meaning it is in fact possible to have a token that accrues almost all value.

The fact that the Venice founders said "well here's a token, and it has this X utility" and didn't promise that it was like equity is irrelevant, they *obviously* benefitted from the unsaid assumption by a ton of market participants that VVV was "potentially the asset that would accrue all the value of the protocol".

That's one of the huge benefits of launching a token, it's that the token will get inflows from buyers who are valuing it like pseudo equity, but then the team is actually completely free to decide what value will really accrue to it.

Given the total asymmetry, the team has a huge incentive to never be transparent about *what the token is and what it's NOT*, and that's what Venice did, and is still doing right now (because they probably don't even know themselves how the value will accrue between token and equity yet.)

Yano didn't want to get into an argument with you but his intuition was perfectly correct that Erik took the advantages of having a token without then taking full responsibility.
Because at the end of the day a team should either launch a token with the goal of accruing all value to it, or simply shouldn't launch a token at all. But a lot of teams want the double dip in crypto because there's no social stigma around it, we're so used to see tokenholders being rugged all the time that it's 'okay' to launch a token which will trend towards zero.
If a team wants to launch a utility token that 'has a very restricted utility and nothing more', then they should be *extremely* unequivocal about it, which was clearly not the case here.

This token<>equity debate is literally the whole shtick of the Blockworks Token Transparency Framework, because they fully understand the problem (which Venice perpetuates), like anyone in crypto who isn't in a conflict of interest.

Just to be clear, I listen to Empire weekly and I love the podcast, I have zero hostility towards you, Rob.

thread_tweets.text:
Maybe in the end they will make sure that a lot of value actually accrues to VVV, we don't know, but it still wouldn't change the fact that they are perpetuating one of the problems that is plaguing crypto. Which is that most teams want the double dip of having both a token and equity, so that they benefit from the liquid public markets and from all the inflows from crypto buyers (including many who will by default value the token as pseudo equity) while keeping the optionality to fully rug the tokenholders.

The fact that we still have no idea about what value is really going to accrue to VVV is the problem. 'Trust me bro' is not a good answer to the equity<>token dual structure, even if it might work out in the end for holders in some situations.

It warms my heart to witness the honorable Frenchman continuing the tradition of great engineers like Bartholdi and Eiffel in constructing magnificent monuments to commemorate freedom in the United States.

This colossal statue stands proudly in Freedom Plaza, Washington, D.C. The plaza itself features a grand marble and granite map of the city plan of 1791, honoring Pierre Charles L’Enfant, another French architect who designed the master plan for the city!

The statue, designed by @AtelierMissor_ , is truly a masterpiece. The Spirit of Liberty gracefully holds the Constitution in her right arm. 

It allows us to celebrate the 250th anniversary of the United States with gratitude and reverence for the enduring spirit of Liberty that continues to inspire people all over the world!
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Our first contribution to the rebeautification of the USA, here in Washington DC.

As Frenchmen, we are honored to serve the friendship between our two great nations. https://t.co/lg4JOETdKc
